item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the kind comment! I definitely had fun developing the containment procedures (my co-author came up with the core anomaly); I'm glad that you found them enjoyable to read.

I hope someone designs a fanart logo for the charity at some point. I'd also enjoy seeing the hat.

I'd love to see that too!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very pleasant SCP, featuring just the right level of weirdness, plus enjoyably unusual yet straightforwardly-justified containment procedures.

I hope someone designs a fanart logo for the charity at some point. I'd also enjoy seeing the hat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree; the Foundation-verse needs to have hopeful stories to balance out the darker aspects. Thank you for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We need more nice anomalies on this site. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
grandma sheep showing she was angry/disappointed in them?

Yep! Grandma Sheep does not approve of the companies those individuals worked for before their Foundation days.

Also, a slight nod to fairytale entities that use the illusory power of "glamour" to present a certain appearance when looked upon by others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what was the significance of grandma sheep looking like giant grandmas for staff who worked in animal testing? grandma sheep showing she was angry/disappointed in them?

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
It makes me happy to know that reading this article helped you feel better. These are pretty strange and stressful times.

I wish you and your grandmother and your family the best, and thank you for reading!

I can’t visit my grandmother right now, and probably won’t be able to for a while because of how the world is at the moment, but this SCP helped me feel better about that. I don’t have a lot to say except thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the kind comment! Incidentally, a lot of the quirky behaviors from Grandma Sheep are based on stories I've heard from my friends over the years about their grandparents (and my own maternal Grandma).

The little note about finding out she liked knitting magazines when she broke into the breakroom and got distracted by some was a nice touch.

Thanks! I like to headcanon that the Foundation is a rather serious workplace, but when personnel are off the clock they have plenty of opportunity for hobbyist and support groups, knitting included. And that's how Grandma Sheep decided that sure, why not, she'll hang out here for a little while longer or so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how the sheep essentially is contained by the foundation doing what she was doing but on a bigger scale, just less anomalous in nature. It really ended up being a great Everyone Wins scenario: Foundation gets to contain the sheep and cover up the odder aspects of her doings, sheep still gets to provide woolen clothing to children who need it around the world, and if sheep escapes, is willing to be recaptured as a "lost mascot".

The little note about finding out she liked knitting magazines when she broke into the breakroom and got distracted by some was a nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Docsigma is correct! Grandma Sheep does what Grandma Sheep wants, and if she wants to poof over to the knitting shop across the country to look at their selection of colored wools, there's nothing the Foundation can do besides track her down and ask her to please go back to containment. As such, Keter-class.

we all need a grandma sheep in our lives

This is very true.

Thank you kindly for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even though it more-or-less is cooperating with the Foundation, the fact remains that it is able to escape containment pretty much any time it chooses to do so.

(+1 btw, we all need a grandma sheep in our lives)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this SCP but why is it keter if it's so safely contained,It should be euclid in my opinion.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Glad you enjoyed the read! I agree that the site could use some more good-natured anomalies amidst the horrors and grimdark.

Thank you kindly for the +1 and nice comment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As Zyn has said already, a Keter SCP is just something that is really hard to contain.

An eldritch entity that has the power to destroy the entire world in a second, but can be contained by just locking it into a room and leaving it there is considered Safe.

A normal soccer ball that has the ability to teleport wherever it wants randomly (but nothing else) is still considered Keter.

Aww, so cute!

This was a great read. It's difficult to find a good-natured SCP these days. The objects and the children's testimonies were the best part.

Definetly a +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you kindly for the nice comment, and the upvote that got us to +100! We're glad you enjoyed the article. :)

What happens to all the woolen gifts if there's some cross-SCP contamination, or what if the sheep is a Trojan … Horse? Trojan Sheep?

Grandma Sheep's effectively an arcane powerful witch so the gifts are probably safe from corruption. So far as Grandma Sheep having her own agenda… yeah, it's definitely a possibility. But the Foundation gave her knitting magazines and organizes her fan letter bulletin board, so she's content to not engage in shenanigans for the time being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An enthusiastic +1, and happy I could be # 100!

I love this SCP! It hits all the marks for a bit of light to make the SCPverse more than a crapsack world on the razor's edge of oblivion; it gives the setting something to lose.

It is adorable.
It makes the world nicer.
The Foundation realized that the best way to contain it was to work with it and minimize the anomalous footprint.

Obviously, this doesn't fit with all versions of the Foundation, but it's such a nice addition.

Of course, a tale subverting it might be fun. What happens to all the woolen gifts if there's some cross-SCP contamination, or what if the sheep is a Trojan … Horse? Trojan Sheep? You get the idea. But I love the SCP as is!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you kindly for the upvote!

making the world a better place, one sad child at a time.

My headcanon is that Grandma Sheep has had her decades of fun being a mighty Sheep Witch and this is her retirement hobby.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought "Keter" was only reserved for SCPs that present an immediate danger to the world or the Foundation.

Per the object classes guide: http://www.scp-wiki.net/object-classes

Keter-class SCPs are anomalies that are exceedingly difficult to contain consistently or reliably, with containment procedures often being extensive and complex. The Foundation often can't contain these SCPs well due to not having a solid understanding of the anomaly, or lacking the technology to properly contain or counter it. A Keter SCP does not mean the SCP is dangerous, just that it is simply very difficult or costly to contain.

Emphasis the last sentence. Granted, this was changed several years back (originally for something to have the Keter object class, there was a requirement for a danger element), so it's understandable that there's still confusion regarding effectively harmless Keters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this article, but one thing about it that really bugged me was that its' class is "Keter" with no explanation. I kept expecting some sort of terrible danger to be revealed, but nothing ever came of it and it just seems like a dangling plot thread. I understand how its' effects could be potentially dangerous or disruptive to the veil, but the same can be said for a lot of SCPs. I thought "Keter" was only reserved for SCPs that present an immediate danger to the world or the Foundation. That's the only thing that bothers me. For now no vote, but if this is changed to "Euclid" or the danger potential is explained in the article then I'll upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well… I'll be damned… a keter that not only isn't actively trying to hurt anyone, but is making the world a better place, one sad child at a time. +1 for a truly unique one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with you—there's got to be some bright spots in the Foundation-verse to keep people's hopes up.

Thank you very much for the upvote! I'm glad you enjoyed the article. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, Yes, YES, A thousand times yes!
This is the type of SCP that you gotta find once in a while to contrast all those other, darker skips, and I love it. Young me would have loved a shirt that made me more self-confident. +1 for wizard grandma sheep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Basically! More on the "witch" side than "fairy". All sheep though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So a fairy godmother witch, in the body of a sheep

love it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Comparing the two I'm surprised I didn't think of 3246. Incidentally, in earlier drafts of this there was originally more focus on Grandma Sheep being a Serpent's Hand affiliate who was on a sort of undercover vacation, but reviewers preferred the version without the SH mention and a bit more info in the table. We didn't want to make this too log-heavy though!

Thank you for the upvote and the nice comment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There really is nothing quite like handmade clothing made by a loving grandma.

Thank you very much for the upvote! I'm glad to hear that you enjoyed the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, this really reminded me of SCP-3246, but after I read it again, I think I like this one more than 3246. It seems more developed than just an "anomalous grandma," and I think they both deserve to stay, despite their obvious parallels.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something about this one, maybe the references to a loving grandma, one's childhood, or just the thought of a warm pair of mittens made me, and as cheesy as it is to say, smile with my soul.

It's an heartfelt +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sheep are great fluffy bois

Also, handmade knitted stuff is super nice

Thank you for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh! Good catch! It's a remnant from a previous edit; originally the gift was a hat but it was switched. The discrepancy has been fixed; thanks for bringing it up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Small note: the last paragraph mentions socks, but then describes an inscription on the inside of a hat. Was that meant to be the witch hat? Or did you switch the little girl''s gift from a hat to socks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i love sheep so goddamn much

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'locations it manifests at' sounds somewhat awkward to me.

Probably the "at" at the end there. I've edited that sentence!

I like the idea of Foundation personnel leading knitting groups of D-Class personnel. I got a laugh out of the mental image of a massive and imposing Agent Skullcrusher, perhaps wearing a pair of glasses too small for his face, sitting around in a circle with several hard-bitten D-class knitting mittens and scarves.

This is the best and it is now my headcanon. I feel like we need more stories about Foundation personnel doing more slice-of-life stuff, since if everything's high-stress and emergencies day in day out no one will want to show up for work. And if the Foundation can contain an ages-old powerful sheep witch with knitting circles… why wouldn't they?

Thank you very much for the comment!

item.title: Re: Jr. Staff Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
I've added an adjective to the sentence:

SCP-5160 has the appearance of a non-anomalous geriatric sheep, but has not shown evidence of further aging.

Hopefully this clears things up!

item.title: Re: Jr. Staff Site Crit
item.content:encoded: Oh, that makes more sense. I think it could be explained a little better, but its still great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it. It's well written and the idea is unique and cute. It strikes a good balance of keeping an entity contained through negotiation without going fully down the path of appeasement. I think there are too few good-natured, heart-warming type articles coming out of the Foundation, so I'm happy to see another one. I like the idea of Foundation personnel leading knitting groups of D-Class personnel. I got a laugh out of the mental image of a massive and imposing Agent Skullcrusher, perhaps wearing a pair of glasses too small for his face, sitting around in a circle with several hard-bitten D-class knitting mittens and scarves.

The only note I have is just a minor one on the wording of a sentence.

SCP-5160 has been fitted with a custom-designed decorative tracking collar to allow for the Foundation to identify the locations it manifests at.

'locations it manifests at' sounds somewhat awkward to me. My suggestion would be something like 'locations in which it manifests'.

item.title: Re: Jr. Staff Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much for the review! Quick question so we know what to edit:

How do people confuse Grandmother sheep for a mascot?

My headcanon is that the designed logo is simple enough that pretty much any regular sheep could work for a meet-and-greet. Give the sheep a hat and that's fine.

I guess if they're less like anthropomorphic, but still. I'm having a hard time visualizing the comparison from the picture shown vs what is discribed.

I'm not sure if it was unclear, but Grandma sheep is just a regular-looking sheep with a nice hat. Should we make that clearer?

item.title: Jr. Staff Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
Hello hello!

I'm Kara, jr. staff for the site crit team!

I really enjoyed this, it was really cute with the grandma sheep. Great job!

+ The crit:
– hide block

Honestly, there really isn't much for me to crit here because it was written so well.

Some points:

- How do people confuse Grandmother sheep for a mascot? I guess if they're less like anthropomorphic, but still. I'm having a hard time visualizing the comparison from the picture shown vs what is discribed.

- I really like the detail of Grandma sheep having the final say with things to her nonprofit.

Honestly, I have nothing but praises for this SCP, great work you two! I can not wait to see what either of you write next.

Thank you for reading this short crit, I'm sorry I couldn't help more. Again, great job!

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to KaraKatt or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review

item.title: Re: CoAuthored
item.content:encoded:
BEEP BEEP GRAMMA SHEEP1

Big, big thanks to all of our reviewers! Please let us know if we've missed a name so we can add it in to properly credit you. We really appreciate the time devoted to helping us get this mainsite-ready! It's been in the works for awhile… since December! http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-12843788/scp-idea:sheep-witch

The image source for the fluffy sheep is here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mouton_d%27Ouessant_180508_1.jpg the image is in the public domain.

The general concept for this is reminiscent of various guardian deities in mythology; sort of the same vibes as this one Russian folklore picture book2 I read about a forest witch who wants to be a grandmother, even though the village fears her because she looks scary. This is a sheep who is by all means a powerful sorceress—and she's happy to more or less mooch off the Foundation because hey, they founded a charity in her name and gave her magazines.

We hope you enjoy the read! To all those who are far from your families now, Grandma Sheep believes in you. <3

Footnotes
1. credit to Skorpt in chat
2. After some searching, I found it! It's Babushka Baba Yaga by Patricia Polacco.

item.title: CoAuthored
item.content:encoded:
This was written By YouEverJust and Zyn

it was greenlit by Zyn and MalyceGraves

and reviewed by people in chat: SharpEmbrace, Noodles, siiva, sasarpillsa, Demonic, Portyanki, DrBleep, Calibri_Bold, DrKeanu, BlueJones
